title: The use of 'literary talent': cultural capital, habitus and competition engagement of Chinese literary youth
abstract: This study explores how Chinese literary youth from diverse social backgrounds mobilize literary cultural capital and multiple habitus in their participation in New Concept Writing Competition (NCWC), a pathway to benefit Gaokao for students with special talents. Drawing on interviews with 15 participants from diverse social backgrounds, it examines their motivations, practices, outcomes and educational trajectories. The study shows class-based differences in the accumulation and activation of literary cultural capital and the mediating role of habitus in writing, reflections and paths. Findings demonstrate the multiple habitus (institutional, literature-related and class) that shape the youths' decisions and actions, and the dissonance in the form of tensions between structure and agency, anticipated aspirations and realistic futures, and ambitions and societal realities. This study contributes to expanding understandings of the role of highbrow culture in complex processes of educational mobility and social reproduction.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
